title: Come to the Rock
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-05-23 - Come to the Rock - 1 Peter 2_4-10 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Old and New Testaments Speak Together
subpoints: 1 Peter 2:4-10 contains two explicit quotes from Isaiah (Isaiah 28:16 and 8:14), a quote from Psalm 118:22, and allusions to Exodus 19 and Hosea 1-2
When New Testament writers under inspiration of the Holy Spirit quote and apply Old Testament prophecy to the church, our Bible becomes much bigger and richer

The church is not a replacement of Israel but its continuation and expansion; the visible people of God has always been the collection of all who profess faith in the Messiah in a particular time and their children
Paul in Galatians 3:8 says the gospel was preached to Abraham (Gen. 12); Galatians 3:29 — "If you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise"

outline.title: Who Is the Rock? Jesus Christ the Living Stone (vv. 4, 6-8)
subpoints: Isaiah 28:16 quoted in verse 6: In the midst of proclaiming curses upon Ephraim (Isa. 28:1), God promises a cornerstone — "Behold, I lay in Zion a stone for a foundation, a tried stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation; whoever believes will not act hastily"

Psalm 118:22 applied in verse 7: "The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone. This was the Lord's doing; it is marvelous in our eyes. This is the day the Lord has made" — this is the day of the resurrection; the day the Lord has made
Isaiah 8:14 applied in verse 8: the one Messiah will be an offense to some — "a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense to both the houses of Israel"
Christ is rejected by men (scorned, despised) but precious and honored by the Father; unlike the lifeless stones of Solomon's temple, Christ is the living stone who gives life

Not believing in Jesus is not a neutral passive state — it is an active rejection of the cornerstone upon whom all life is built; if you do not fall upon that rock, the rock will fall upon you (cf. Matt. 21:44)

outline.title: Who Are the People? Living Stones, a Royal Priesthood (vv. 4-5, 9-10)
subpoints: Verses 4-5: coming to the living stone, believers are also built up as living stones — a spiritual house, a holy priesthood; no command, only description: this is what Christians do and what happens to them
Verse 9: a chosen generation (race of the second Adam), a royal priesthood (after the order of Melchizedek), a holy nation (cf. Exodus 19:6), His own special people

The church is the continuation of Israel: Peter applies Old Testament Israel passages to Gentile believers because by faith they are "Abraham's seed and heirs" (Gal. 3:29) and members of "the commonwealth of Israel" (Eph. 2:12)
Hosea 1-2 allusion in verse 10: "Who once were not a people but are now the people of God; who had not obtained mercy but now have obtained mercy" — Gentiles brought in through faith in Christ

As priests, we have direct access to the Father through Christ our mediator — no human mediator needed; the throne of grace is open; unlike Isaiah who cried "woe is me" at the vision of God (Isa. 6), we call God our Father and may even metaphorically crawl into His lap by faith

outline.title: What Do the People Do? Offer Spiritual Sacrifices (v. 5, 9)
subpoints: As a royal priesthood, we offer spiritual sacrifices — not material ones; several examples from Scripture:

Sacrifice 1 — The giving of ourselves completely to God's service (Romans 12:1: "present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable to God"; Calvin's motto: "My heart I give you, Lord, promptly and sincerely")

Sacrifice 2 — Giving financially to enable the spread of the gospel (Philippians 4:18: the Philippians' gift is "a sweet-smelling aroma, an acceptable sacrifice, well pleasing to God"; how we spend our time and money reveals what is in our hearts)

Sacrifice 3 — Singing praises (Hebrews 13:15: "the sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to His name"; there is no such category in the Bible as a Christian who does not sing)
Sacrifice 4 — Doing good and sharing possessions (Hebrews 13:16: "with such sacrifices God is well pleased")
Sacrifice 5 — Proclaiming the praises of the one who brought us out of darkness into light (v. 9); talking to people about what Christ has done is a spiritual sacrifice

outline.title: Conclusion: Keep Coming to Jesus
subpoints: The repeated call of the passage is not a command but a description: coming to Him is what a Christian does — keep coming, you are being built up; "keep coming" is better translated as an ongoing present action
Come to Jesus for salvation, sanctification, growth, change, contentment, joy, marriage, singleness, youth, old age, health, infirmity — for your all
God, who commanded light to shine out of darkness, has shone into our hearts; we who were not a people are now the people of God; we who had not obtained mercy have now obtained mercy

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from 1 Peter 2:4–10, a passage dense with Old Testament quotations and allusions — two from Isaiah (28:16 and 8:14), one from Psalm 118:22, and allusions to Exodus 19 and Hosea 1–2. The sermon's governing call is simple: keep coming to Jesus, the living stone. The passage contains no imperatives, only descriptions — because coming to the Rock is what Christians characteristically do; as they keep coming, they are built up.

The sermon opens with a careful treatment of the church as continuation and expansion of Israel, not its replacement. Peter applies Old Testament Israel passages to largely Gentile churches because by faith they are Abraham's seed and heirs (Galatians 3:29), members of the commonwealth of Israel through the blood of Christ (Ephesians 2:12–13). The Rock promised by God in the midst of Isaiah's woes (Isaiah 28:16) is Jesus Christ. The stone rejected by the builders (Psalm 118:22) is the resurrected Christ — and "this is the day the Lord has made" is the day of resurrection. The stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14) is the same Christ who is an offense to the disobedient. Not believing in Jesus is not a passive neutral state; it is an active rejection that will result in being crushed under the stone that falls.

Those who do come to the Rock become living stones themselves, built up into a spiritual house and a holy priesthood. Peter applies the identity language of Old Testament Israel — chosen generation, royal priesthood, holy nation, God's own people — to the Gentile churches, and the Hosea allusion in verse 10 captures the miracle: those who were once not a people are now the people of God. As priests with direct access to the Father through Christ our one mediator, believers offer spiritual sacrifices: the giving of themselves (Romans 12:1), financial giving for the gospel (Philippians 4:18), singing praises (Hebrews 13:15), doing good and sharing (Hebrews 13:16), and proclaiming the praises of the one who called them out of darkness.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading — 1 Peter 2:4–10

Coming to Him as to a living stone, rejected indeed by men, but chosen by God and precious, you also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house, a holy priesthood, to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ. Therefore it is also contained in the Scripture, "Behold, I lay in Zion a chief cornerstone, elect, precious, and he who believes on Him will by no means be put to shame." Therefore, to you who believe, He is precious; but to those who are disobedient, "The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone," and "A stone of stumbling and a rock of offense." They stumble, being disobedient to the word, to which they also were appointed. But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, His own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light, who once were not a people but are now the people of God, who had not obtained mercy but now have obtained mercy.

Let us pray. Father, I pray that you would speak to us. Pray that you would strengthen me to proclaim your Word faithfully and open all of our hearts to receive your Word. For ask in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Old and New Testaments Speak Together

As we read this portion of First Peter, I hope you notice all the quotes and allusions to the Old Testament. There are two quotes from the prophecy of Isaiah and a quote from the Psalms, and then there are two allusions — one to Exodus 19 and another one to Hosea chapters 1 and 2. When New Testament writers, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, grab the prophecies and the text of the Old Covenant and apply them to us as members of the New Covenant — it is because all of the Bible speaks of one God, who gathers His one people through the one Messiah of God's elect. And today, as we read this passage, that one God is telling you: keep on coming to Jesus, the Rock of your life. That is the point here — keep on coming to Jesus, the Rock of your life.

The New Covenant and the church are not a replacement for Israel. We are often falsely accused of that. The church is not a replacement. The church is a continuation and expansion of Israel. The visible people of God has always been the collection of all who profess faith in the Messiah in a particular time and their children. In the covenant that God made with Abraham — in which God instituted and organized a community that was going to faithfully proclaim the Word of God, administer the sacraments, and preserve itself by the practice of discipline — God said that all the nations would be blessed in Abraham (Genesis 12).

The Apostle Paul grabs hold of that idea in Galatians 3:8: "The Scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the Gentiles by faith, preached the gospel to Abraham beforehand, saying, 'In you all the nations shall be blessed.'" And because of that covenant and because of that promise, all who believe in Christ are Abraham's seed and heirs. As Paul says in Galatians 3:29: "If you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise."

Peter understood that. And in writing to mostly Gentile churches — the internal evidence leads us to that conclusion — he applies passages that were given to the Old Covenant people of God to those who by ethnicity were not Jews but by faith were full members of the Israel of God. That is why it is so appropriate to interpret these three Old Testament passages as fulfilled in the New Covenant.

## Who Is the Rock? Jesus Christ the Living Stone (vv. 4, 6-8)

In verse 6, Peter tells us that the rock God promised in the Old Testament is Jesus Christ Himself. He does that by quoting Isaiah 28.

In Isaiah 28, God is proclaiming curses — woes — upon His own people. "Woe to the crown of pride, to the drunkards of Ephraim, whose glorious beauty is a fading flower" (v. 1). And in the midst of that proclamation of judgment and woe, there is this promise of the rock: "Therefore thus says the Lord God: 'Behold, I lay in Zion a stone for a foundation, a tried stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation; whoever believes will not act hastily'" (v. 16).

Peter saw that in the curse to Israel, there is a promise to the church. And that promise was the Lord Jesus Christ.

And he does not stop there. He sees that the sweet psalmist of Israel prophesied of the glorious ministry of Christ — even in the resurrection — in Psalm 118. He applies that to us today in verse 7. Psalm 118 says: "The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone. This was the Lord's doing; it is marvelous in our eyes. This is the day that the Lord has made; we will rejoice and be glad in it." This day — the Lord's Day — marked by the resurrection of Christ, is the day the Lord has made. And we are glad in it because in it we see the Rock, the Lord Jesus Christ, who is the chief cornerstone upon which we are being built.

Peter continues in verse 8. He uses Isaiah 8:14, telling us that the one Messiah that the one God is sending to gather the one people will be an offense to some: "He will be as a sanctuary, but a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense to both the houses of Israel, as a trap and a snare to the inhabitants of Jerusalem." That Savior — the Lord Jesus Christ — is going to be offensive to some. He is going to cause them to stumble.

Why am I going through this? Because when we see things the way Peter sees them — by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, who guarantees that his interpretation is 100% accurate — our Bible becomes so much bigger and richer and full of wonders. We often think that this blank page in the middle of the Bible keeps the two sides apart from each other, as if they cannot talk. And yet Peter himself speaks from both sides of the Bible — and in doing that, he gives us an amazing, unified Word of God that we can benefit from.

The same Jesus who is the precious cornerstone for those who believe is a stone of stumbling for those who do not. To you who believe, He is precious and honored. To those who are disobedient — the word *disobedient* here is perhaps better translated "unbelieving" — the stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone, and He is a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense.

Not believing in Jesus is not a passive neutral state. It is an active rejection of the one upon whom all abundant life is built — a rejection of the cornerstone upon which all life is built. For those who do not believe in Jesus, He is a stumbling stone. And if you do not fall upon that rock — the Lord Jesus Christ — that rock is going to fall upon you. Either you are broken by falling upon the rock of the grace of God and coming to Him, or you will be crushed under the judgment of the rock falling upon you.

Peter says in verse 8 that unbelievers are disobedient to the word — specifically to the command to believe. We are all everywhere commanded to believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. But even these unbelievers are in the sovereign plan of God; they are appointed by God to unbelief. We will see more of that on Wednesday. So brothers and sisters, even concerning the unbeliever — it is with great urgency that I plead with you: come to Jesus.

## Who Are the People? Living Stones, a Royal Priesthood (vv. 4-5, 9-10)

In verses 4 and 5 and 9, Peter does something remarkable. He applies the identity language of Old Testament Israel to the Gentile churches of Asia Minor.

"Coming to Him as to a living stone... you also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house, a holy priesthood, to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ" (vv. 4–5). And then verse 9: "But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, His own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light."

Notice that in these verses there is no command — only statements of truth, descriptions of who the Christian is and what the Christian does. *Coming to Him* is what a Christian does. Better translated: *you are coming*, or even better, *you keep coming*. You are being built up. That is what happens as you keep coming to the Rock. The life of the Christian is a life of coming to Jesus. He already told us in verse 3 that we know the Lord is good — so keep coming.

Come to Jesus for your salvation. Come to Jesus for your sanctification. Come to Jesus for your growth. Come to Jesus for change. Come to Him for everything. Come to Jesus all the time.

And who is this Jesus that we are to keep coming to? Verse 4 tells us He is rejected by men — scorned, despised, attacked; His message considered foolish. But the same Jesus who is rejected by men is at the end of verse 4 *precious* to God — literally *honored*. He is the living stone who gives life. He is not like the lifeless stones in the temple of the Old Testament. However enormous and impressive those were — and if you have been to Jerusalem and stood at the Western Wall and seen the foundation stones of Solomon's temple, you wonder how they could have moved such stones — those were lifeless stones. The Lord Jesus Christ is the living stone. He is the one who gives life: "I have come that they may have life, and that they may have it more abundantly."

As we keep coming to Him, we too become living stones — we become part of the building of God's house with one another. Each one of us is one of those stones being built together into the great house of God, the church of the living God. And as Peter tells us in verse 5, as we keep on coming to Him, we are not only the temple — we are also the priests.

In verses 5 and 9, all of us are part of this great priesthood — not just any priesthood, but a royal priesthood, after the order of Melchizedek, just like our High Priest the Lord Jesus Christ. We are a chosen race — the race of the second Adam, the Lord Jesus Christ. We are a holy nation, the commonwealth of Christ. See that in verse 9, which alludes to Exodus 19:6, where the Lord said to Israel: "You shall be to Me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation." Israel failed to be that. But through faith we become that holy nation. To the point that the Apostle Paul, in Ephesians chapter 2, says that you who were far off from the commonwealth of Israel — the blood of Christ has now made you near, made you members of that commonwealth of Israel.

And best of all, brothers and sisters, we have been called out of darkness and brought into the wonderful light of Christ. As Paul says: "For it is the God who commanded light to shine out of darkness, who has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ" (2 Cor. 4:6).

And verse 10 gives us the Hosea allusion — Hosea chapters 1 and 2: "Who once were not a people but are now the people of God, who had not obtained mercy but now have obtained mercy." You and I — we are the people of God. We are the objects of the mercy of God. And not only that, we have direct access to God because of the Lord Jesus Christ. We are a royal priesthood. We do not need another mediator. As the Apostle Paul says: "For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus" (1 Timothy 2:5).

Brothers and sisters, we can pray, we can come before the throne of grace, we can approach God without fear and with confidence, because we have access to Him. He is not going to turn us away, because we are all priests through the Lord Jesus Christ. You do not need to go to somebody else. You do not need to have another human mediator. Christ is sufficient, and because of Him we are even now in the presence of the Lord Almighty.

We do not have the same reaction as Isaiah did. Remember when Isaiah saw God in that vision — Isaiah 6 — he cried, "Woe is me, for I am undone!" That same throne Isaiah was terrified of is now for us what Peter calls a throne of grace — and we can come to our Father. We can even crawl into His lap as it were, by faith, knowing that He never rejects us, because we all have free access to Him through the Lord Jesus Christ.

This is the doctrine known as the priesthood of all believers.

## What Do the People Do? Offer Spiritual Sacrifices (v. 5, 9)

And as priests, we offer sacrifices — spiritual ones. Look at verse 5: "You also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house, a holy priesthood, to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ." And verse 9 says we are to "proclaim the praises of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light."

The Scriptures use the term "spiritual sacrifice" in several ways:

**First — We offer ourselves completely to God's service.** The Apostle Paul says in Romans 12:1: "I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable to God, which is your reasonable service." John Calvin used to say that his motto for life was: "My heart I give you, Lord, promptly and sincerely." That is the kind of sacrifice we are to give to the Lord — our hearts given to the Lord promptly and sincerely.

**Second — Giving financially to enable the spread of the gospel.** That does not sound very spiritual — I know what you're thinking, the pastor is trying to increase the offering. But the Bible says this. Giving to the church is a measure of spiritual growth. In Philippians 4:18, after the Philippians sent a gift to Paul in his poverty, he responds: "I have all and abound. I am full, having received from Epaphroditus the things sent from you, a sweet-smelling aroma, an acceptable sacrifice, well pleasing to God." Their gift was a sacrifice well pleasing to God. A wise deacon once told me: "If you give me somebody's calendar and somebody's pocketbook — where they keep a record of how they've spent their money — I can tell you what's important to them." How we spend our time and how we spend our money tells others what is important to us and reflects what is in our hearts.

**Third — The singing of praises.** In Hebrews 13:15: "Therefore by Him let us continually offer the sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to His name." Do you know something that does not exist — at least not in the Bible? A Christian who does not sing. That category does not exist in the Bible because singing praises is intrinsic to being a Christian. It is one of the sacrifices we offer as we keep coming to Jesus and are being built up. Now, that does not mean you all have to be in the choir — some of you may be asked not to be in the choir. But in the worship of God, those are sweet-smelling praises to the Lord. It does not matter how bad your voice is. We offer that to the Lord.

**Fourth — Doing good and sharing our possessions.** Hebrews 13:16: "But do not forget to do good and to share, for with such sacrifices God is well pleased."

**Fifth — Proclaiming the praises of the one who brought us out of darkness into light** (verse 9). Actually talking to people about what Christ has done is a spiritual sacrifice that we offer to Him.

These are representative examples to help us see that anything we do in the service of God — from hearts that have come to Jesus — can be seen as spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God. These do not earn us any merit; we are not trying to get God to forgive us or anything like that, because Jesus has already done that for us. But they are the natural consequence of being built up in Christ.

Peter says the Christians are those who keep coming to Jesus. And as they keep coming to Jesus, they are being built up — and the necessary result of being built up is that they offer spiritual sacrifices. It is just the natural thing of the Christian life.

## Conclusion: Keep Coming to Jesus

In the midst of these glorious truths about those who have come to Jesus, Peter inserts the sobering reality of those who do not believe — verses 7 and 8 as we have seen. The stone which the builders rejected is a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense to those who are disobedient to the word.

So brothers and sisters, come to Jesus. Keep on coming because He is the foundation upon whom all of life is built.

Come to Jesus for your salvation. Come to Jesus for your sanctification. Come to Jesus for your contentment. Come to Jesus for your joy. Come to Jesus for your marriage. Come to Jesus in your singleness. Come to Jesus in your youth. Come to Jesus in your old age. Come to Jesus in your health. Come to Jesus in your infirmities. Come to Jesus for your all. Come to Him and keep on coming, as you are being built up upon that holy living stone that is being built together.

Father in heaven, thank you that you have given us the Lord Jesus Christ, the chief cornerstone of the church. We thank you that we are being built upon Him and that you are working in us to offer spiritual sacrifices to you. Thank you for the words of the Apostle Peter. We pray that you would write them in our hearts that we might live according to them. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
